eras.name: Sarmatian Noble Fashion
eras.description: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth nobles adopted an orientalizing aesthetic inspired by Central Asian steppe cultures. The 'kontusz' sash and shaved head with a single topknot became hallmarks of the uniquely Polish 'Sarmatian' identity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 17th-century Polish Sarmatian nobleman in a richly embroidered crimson żupan under a flowing gold kontusz, wearing a wide silk pas kontuszowy sash, fur-trimmed cap, and curved szabla saber, in a Baroque manor hall.

eras.name: Partition-Era Folk Resistance
eras.description: During the partitions, traditional folk dress became a form of silent resistance against Prussian, Russian, and Austrian cultural domination. Regional costumes from Łowicz, Kraków, and Podhale preserved Polish identity through vibrant embroidery.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 19th-century Polish peasant couple from the Łowicz region: the woman in a hand-embroidered floral blouse with a vivid striped skirt, the man in a white linen shirt and embroidered vest, standing in a field of poppies.

eras.name: Interwar Art Deco Elegance
eras.description: Newly independent Poland embraced European Art Deco while maintaining folk motifs. Warsaw and Kraków became fashion centers where Parisian couture was adapted with distinctly Polish textile patterns and craftsmanship.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1930s Art Deco fashion portrait in Warsaw: an elegant Polish woman in a bias-cut emerald satin evening gown with fox fur stole, Art Deco geometric jewelry, standing in a modernist café interior.

eras.name: Communist-Era DIY Fashion
eras.description: Under communism, severe material shortages forced extraordinary creativity. Home sewing was universal, and women recreated Western styles from smuggled magazines. The 'Moda Polska' state fashion house attempted to create a socialist couture.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A resourceful 1970s Polish woman in Warsaw wearing a stylish homemade dress copied from a Western fashion magazine, paired with locally made leather shoes, standing by a brutalist apartment building.

eras.name: Post-Solidarity Transformation
eras.description: The fall of communism unleashed a flood of Western fashion influences. Open-air markets (bazaars) became fashion hubs selling imported and counterfeit goods, while the first Polish fashion designers began establishing independent labels.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A bustling 1990s Polish open-air bazaar with rows of stalls selling leather jackets, jeans, and imported accessories, young shoppers in oversized denim and colorful windbreakers examining goods.

eras.name: The New Polish Fashion
eras.description: Łódź, once a textile manufacturing powerhouse, has reinvented itself as Poland's fashion capital. A new generation of designers combines folk heritage with avant-garde aesthetics, gaining recognition at international fashion weeks.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A contemporary Polish fashion editorial in a converted Łódź textile factory: a model in a deconstructed white wool coat with folk embroidery accents, industrial brick walls and steel beams in background.

textiles.name: Pas Kontuszowy (Sash Belt)
textiles.description: Elaborately woven silk sash belts up to 4 meters long, featuring Persian-inspired floral patterns, originally imported from the Ottoman Empire and later produced in Poland.
textiles.technique: Double-sided silk tapestry weaving with metallic gold and silver threads

textiles.name: Łowicz Cut-Out (Wycinanki)
textiles.description: Vibrant paper cut-out designs traditionally used for decorating interiors, now adapted as textile prints and embroidery patterns in contemporary fashion.
textiles.technique: Layered paper cutting adapted to textile appliqué and screen printing

textiles.name: Highland Wool (Sukno Góralskie)
textiles.description: Thick, naturally water-resistant wool woven by Góral highlanders, traditionally used for trousers, vests, and cloaks in the Tatra Mountains.
textiles.technique: Hand-spun mountain sheep wool, woven on narrow looms and fulled by hand

textiles.name: Silesian Lace
textiles.description: Fine bobbin lace produced in the industrial region of Silesia, influenced by both Germanic and Bohemian lacemaking traditions.
textiles.technique: Bobbin lace with geometric patterns on a cylindrical pillow

climate.fashionImplication: Cold, snowy winters demand heavy wool coats, fur accessories, and robust leather boots. The short but warm summers allow for lighter linen and cotton. Layering is essential due to dramatic seasonal temperature swings.

culturalInfluences: Partition-era cultural resistance through folk dress
The Solidarity movement and worker identity
Regional folk diversity (Góral, Łowicz, Kaszuby, Silesian)
